corpus_building.implemented_as: The TXT paper is converted into a section-aware annotated mini-corpus.

tokenization.implemented_as: Every concept phrase and sentence is normalized into tokens for counts and n-grams.

normalization.implemented_as: Lower-casing and punctuation cleanup are used for phrase matching and deduplication.

n_gram.implemented_as: Concept length and document top unigrams/bigrams/trigrams are exposed.

bow_and_naive_bayes.implemented_as: Section BoW hits act as class-conditional section evidence for intro/method/experiment/conclusion style decisions.

tf_idf_document_term_matrix.implemented_as: Document-level frequency and TF-IDF matrices expose which section concepts are characteristic of this paper.

laplace_smoothing.implemented_as: P(section | concept) uses add-one smoothing, so unseen section-concept pairs are not forced to zero.

evidence_cue_lexicon.implemented_as: Cue phrases such as propose, achieve, outperform, and BLEU give transparent rule-based evidence hints.

sequence_labeling.implemented_as: The first model contributes BIO boundary evidence for concept phrase extraction.

canonicalization_noisy_channel_style.implemented_as: Observed surface phrases are mapped to canonical terms with an alias confidence score.

location.sentence: Batch gradient descent also does not allow us to update our model online , i.e. with new examples on-the-fly.

context_window.previous: 2.1 Batch gradient descent Vanilla gradient descent, aka batch gradient descent, computes the gradient of the cost function w.r.t. to the parameters θ for the entire training dataset: θ = θ − η · ∇ θ J ( θ ) (1) As we need to calculate the gradients for the whole dataset to perform just one update, batch gradient descent can be very slow and is intractable for datasets that do not fit in memory.
context_window.current: Batch gradient descent also does not allow us to update our model online , i.e. with new examples on-the-fly.
context_window.next: In code, batch gradient descent looks something like this: for i in range ( nb_epochs ): params_grad = evaluate_gradient ( loss_function , data , params ) params = params - learning_rate * params_grad For a pre-defined number of epochs, we first compute the gradient vector params_grad of the loss function for the whole dataset w.r.t. our parameter vector params .

location.sentence: 2 Figure 1: SGD fluctuation (Source: Wikipedia) 2.3 Mini-batch gradient descent Mini-batch gradient descent finally takes the best of both worlds and performs an update for every mini-batch of n training examples: θ = θ − η · ∇ θ J ( θ ; x ( i : i + n ) ; y ( i : i + n ) ) (3) This way, it a) reduces the variance of the parameter updates, which can lead to more stable conver- gence; and b) can make use of highly optimized matrix optimizations common to state-of-the-art deep learning libraries that make computing the gradient w.r.t. a mini-batch very efficient.

location.sentence: To realize this, they first define another exponentially decaying average, this time not of squared gradients but of squared parameter updates: E [∆ θ 2 ] t = γE [∆ θ 2 ] t − 1 + (1 − γ )∆ θ 2 t (15) 6 The root mean squared error of parameter updates is thus: RM S [∆ θ ] t = √ E [∆ θ 2 ] t +  (16) Since RM S [∆ θ ] t is unknown, we approximate it with the RMS of parameter updates until the previous time step.

context_window.previous: For clarity, we now rewrite our vanilla SGD update in terms of the parameter update vector ∆ θ t : ∆ θ t = − η · g t,i θ t +1 = θ t + ∆ θ t (11) The parameter update vector of Adagrad that we derived previously thus takes the form: ∆ θ t = − η √ G t +  g t (12) We now simply replace the diagonal matrix G t with the decaying average over past squared gradients E [ g 2 ] t : ∆ θ t = − η √ E [ g 2 ] t +  g t (13) As the denominator is just the root mean squared (RMS) error criterion of the gradient, we can replace it with the criterion short-hand: ∆ θ t = − η RM S [ g ] t g t (14) The authors note that the units in this update (as well as in SGD, Momentum, or Adagrad) do not match, i.e. the update should have the same hypothetical units as the parameter.
context_window.current: To realize this, they first define another exponentially decaying average, this time not of squared gradients but of squared parameter updates: E [∆ θ 2 ] t = γE [∆ θ 2 ] t − 1 + (1 − γ )∆ θ 2 t (15) 6 The root mean squared error of parameter updates is thus: RM S [∆ θ ] t = √ E [∆ θ 2 ] t +  (16) Since RM S [∆ θ ] t is unknown, we approximate it with the RMS of parameter updates until the previous time step.
context_window.next: Replacing the learning rate η in the previous update rule with RM S [∆ θ ] t − 1 finally yields the Adadelta update rule: ∆ θ t = − RM S [∆ θ ] t − 1 RM S [ g ] t g t θ t +1 = θ t + ∆ θ t (17) With Adadelta, we do not even need to set a default learning rate, as it has been eliminated from the update rule.

top_candidates.sentence: In the course of this overview, we look at different variants of gradient descent, summarize challenges, introduce the most common optimization algorithms, review architectures in a parallel and distributed setting, and investigate additional strategies for optimizing gradient descent.
At the same time, every state-of-the-art Deep Learning library contains implementations of various algorithms to optimize gradient descent (e.g. lasagne's 2 , caffe's 3 , and keras' 4 documentation).

Subsequently, in Section 4, we will introduce the most common optimization algorithms by showing their motivation to resolve these challenges and how this leads to the derivation of their update rules.
Depending on the amount of data, we make a trade-off between the accuracy of the parameter update and the time it takes to perform an update.
Kingma et al. show that its bias-correction helps Adam slightly outperform RMSprop towards the end of optimization as gradients become sparser.

As has been shown, SGD usually achieves to find a minimum, but it might take significantly longer than with some of the optimizers, is much more reliant on a robust initialization and annealing schedule, and may get stuck in saddle points rather than local minima.

downstream_contract.intended_use: Feed this JSON to a later local open-source model to write an overview.
